coredata.dc:title: Boosting the photogenerated hole separation and injection of Ti-Fe2O3 by co-modifying carbon quantum dots and NiFe layered double hydroxide
coredata.dc:creator: Bu, Qijing
Zhao, Qifeng
Sun, Gaohao
Liu, Qingyun
Xie, Tengfeng
coredata.dc:description: Hematite (α-Fe2O3) as a photoanode material has attracted widespread attention in photoelectrochemical (PEC) water oxidation reaction. However, its performance is still impeded by the severe recombination of photogenerated charges in the bulk photoanode and at the photoanode/electrolyte interface. Herein, we have fabricated an integrated photoanode consisted of Ti doped α-Fe2O3 (Ti-Fe2O3), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and NiFe layered double hydroxide (NiFe-LDH), aiming at improving the injection efficiency as well as the separation efficiency. The results demonstrate the appearance of CQDs in NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 improves the photogenerated hole extraction from Ti-Fe2O3 to NiFe-LDH, while NiFe-LDH as a cocatalyst guarantees the rapid transfer of photogenerated holes at the photoanode/electrolyte interface. Under the synergistic effect of CQDs and NiFe-LDH, NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 exhibits a stable photocurrent density of 2.89 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is much higher than that of Ti-Fe2O3.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Separation efficiency
Injection efficiency
Carbon quantum dots
NiFe-LDH
α-Fe2O3

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PEC performance of Ti-Fe2O3 and Ti-Fe2O3 with cocatalyst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FE-SEM images of Ti-Fe2O3 (a) and NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 (b). TEM image (c) and HRTEM image (d) of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3.
XPS spectra of C 1s (a), O 1s (b), Ni 2p (c) and Fe 2p (d). All results are from NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3.

(a) Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes under dark and simulated AM 1.5 G solar light illumination. (b) Applied bias photon-to-current efficiencies (ABPE) of all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes. (c) Incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) of all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes. (d) Photocurrent stability test of all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes at 1.23 V vs. RHE.

(a) SPV spectra of all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes with a chopping frequency of 23 Hz. (b) SPV transient measurement of all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes under 355 nm pulse laser irradiation. (c) Separation efficiency of all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes.

(a) The transient current-potential curves of all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes under chopped illumination. The frequency is 0.125 Hz. (b) Accumulated charge densities of all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes as a function of applied potential. (c) Injection efficiency of all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes.
(a) Nyquist plots of all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes at 1 V vs. RHE under AM 1.5 G illumination. (b) The equivalent circuit.
The fitted results of Nyquist plots for all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes.

head.ce:title: Boosting the photogenerated hole separation and injection of Ti-Fe2O3 by co-modifying carbon quantum dots and NiFe layered double hydroxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Biological Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Mining Disaster Prevention and Control Co-founded by Shandong Province and the Ministry of Science and Technology, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266590, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Biological Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Mining Disaster Prevention and Control Co-founded by Shandong Province and the Ministry of Science and Technology, Shandong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical and Biological Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Mining Disaster Prevention and Control Co-founded by Shandong Province and the Ministry of Science and Technology, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266590, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hematite (α-Fe2O3) as a photoanode material has attracted widespread attention in photoelectrochemical (PEC) water oxidation reaction. However, its performance is still impeded by the severe recombination of photogenerated charges in the bulk photoanode and at the photoanode/electrolyte interface. Herein, we have fabricated an integrated photoanode consisted of Ti doped α-Fe2O3 (Ti-Fe2O3), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and NiFe layered double hydroxide (NiFe-LDH), aiming at improving the injection efficiency as well as the separation efficiency. The results demonstrate the appearance of CQDs in NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 improves the photogenerated hole extraction from Ti-Fe2O3 to NiFe-LDH, while NiFe-LDH as a cocatalyst guarantees the rapid transfer of photogenerated holes at the photoanode/electrolyte interface. Under the synergistic effect of CQDs and NiFe-LDH, NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 exhibits a stable photocurrent density of 2.89 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is much higher than that of Ti-Fe2O3.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting for hydrogen has been regarded as a promising technology to solve the current energy crisis in industrial development [1,2]. However, great challenges are still met in this process because oxygen evolution reaction (OER) requires four electrons with a significant kinetics difficulty [3]. Therefore, the development of photoanodes with high performance and low cheap is urgent for PEC water splitting. High-active photoanodes should contain the following points: good light harvesting capability, efficient separation of photogenerated charges and quick injection of photogenerated holes [4]. Hematite (α-Fe2O3) as a photoanode has been considered as a strong contender because of its desirable band gap with 2.1 eV and outstanding theoretical maximum photocurrent of 12.6 mA/cm2 [5,6]. Unfortunately, the experimental photocurrent density of α-Fe2O3 is far below the theoretical value of 12.6 mA/cm2, which is resulted from the severe recombination of photogenerated charges in the bulk photoanode and sluggish water oxidation kinetics [7,8]. Therefore, building composite photoanodes which satisfy the criteria of high separation efficiency of photogenerated charges and quick injection of photogenerated holes is essential for improving PEC performance of α-Fe2O3.

Generally, introducing a promising electrocatalyst (cocatalyst) is an effective method to accelerate the water oxidation kinetics [9], such as CoPi [10], CoNi-MOFs [11], Ni2P [12], cobalt polyoxometalate [13], NiFeOx [14], FeOOH [15], and layered double hydroxides (LDHs) [16]. LDHs, especially NiFe-LDH, have attracted a lot of attention due to their advantages of inexpensive, high performance of electrocatalytic water oxidation and self-healing function [17]. Recently, NiFe-LDH modified semiconductors are successfully constructed to boost the PEC performance, for example, NiFe-LDH/α-Fe2O3 [18], NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 [19,20], and NiFe-LDH/WO3 [21]. Unfortunately, although the transfer of photogenerated holes at the photoanode/electrolyte interface is obviously expedited after the deposition of NiFe-LDH, the separation efficiency of photogenerated charges is unsatisfactory, which limits the PEC performance of the abovementioned photoanodes. To tackle the drawback, constructing heterojunctions with matched band positions is generally used to boost the photogenerated charge separation in the bulk photoanodes [22,23]. For example, Chunjoong Kim et al. have synthesized WO3/BiVO4/CoP for PEC water splitting, in which the type-II WO3/BiVO4 heterojunction facilitates the bulk charge transport [24]. Besides, incorporating the conducting medium (graphene [25], carbon quantum dots [26] and MXene [27]) with semiconductor is also effective to ameliorate the separation efficiency of photogenerated charges. In particular, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been combined with various semiconductors (e.g., TiO2, Fe2O3 and BiVO4), which induce the photogenerated holes to transport from semiconductors to CQDs, and thus improve the performance of composite photoanodes [6,28,29].

Inspired by the above discussion, we have attempt to design a composite photoanode to facilitate the separation and transfer of photogenerated charges simultaneously, which is consisted of Ti-Fe2O3, CQDs and NiFe-LDH. The resultant NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 photoanode exhibits a photocurrent density of 2.89 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is much higher than that of Ti-Fe2O3. Furthermore, the results of separation efficiency and injection efficiency indicate that CQDs and NiFe-LDH play indispensable roles in the PEC performance of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 photoanode.

Firstly, Ti doped α-Fe2O3 nanorod array (Ti-Fe2O3) was fabricated on the top of FTO glass substrate according to a hydrothermal process [30,31]. The precursor solution of Ti-Fe2O3 was comprised of 0.15 M iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3.6H2O), 1 M sodium nitrate (NaNO3), and 1 mL 0.05% titanium tetrachloride (TiCl4) ethanol solution (volume percentage). 33 mL of the precursor solution was added into the Teflon-lined autoclave, in which the cleaned FTO was putted with its conducting side facing down. Subsequently, the Teflon-lined autoclave was heated at 100 °C for 12 h at an oven to obtain the yellow Ti-FeOOH film. The Ti-FeOOH film was washed completely with deionized water and calcined at 550 °C for 2 h to obtain Ti-Fe2O3.

Secondly, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were fabricated through a pyrolysis method reported previously [6]. 20 mL aqueous solution contained 0.07 M sucrose was placed into the Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 5 h. After natural cooling, CQDs were achieved by drawing the upper clear solution. Whereafter, the simple spin-coating method was utilized to deposit CQDs on Ti-Fe2O3 (named as C/Ti-Fe2O3). It should be pointed out that the volume was varied (100 μL, 200 μL, 400 μL) to optimize the PEC performance.

Finally, a thin film of NiFe-LDH was electrodeposited on Ti-Fe2O3 and C/Ti-Fe2O3 at a constant current density of − 0.1 mA/cm2 in a three-electrode configuration, in which Ti-Fe2O3 or C/Ti-Fe2O3, Pt wire and Ag/AgCl were regard as the working electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The electrolyte was composed of FeCl2.4H2O and Ni(NO3)2.6H2O, and the total molar concentration was 0.1 M. The obtained electrode was designated as NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3. Similarly, the electrodeposition time and the molar ratio of Ni(NO3)2.6H2O to FeCl2.4H2O were optimized.

The crystalline structure, morphology and chemical element composition of all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes were measured by X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, scanning electron microscope (SEM, S4800, 5 kV), transmission electron microscopy (TEM and HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The optical absorption properties and band gap energies of all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes were characterized with the help of UV–vis diffusion absorption spectra (UV–vis DRS). The separation efficiency and the kinetic behavior of photogenerated charges were explored through the surface photovoltage (SPV) spectra based on the lock-in amplifier and SPV transient measurement.

All PEC measurements were carried out by the standard three-electrode system, in which the light intensity was 100 mW/cm2 provided by 300 W xenon lamp equipped with an AM 1.5 G fitter. The Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes were served as the working electrode, Pt wire was served as the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl electrode was served as the reference electrode. The electrolyte was 1 M KOH with or without 0.5 M H2O2, and the surface area of photoanodes that was contacted with the electrolyte was 0.283 cm2. The applied potentials versus Ag/AgCl were converted to the potentials versus RHE by the equation:
E
RHE
=
E
Ag
/
AgCl
+ 0.059 pH +
E
Ag
/
AgCl
θ
. The incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) of all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes was investigated at 1.23 V vs. RHE under 300 W xenon lamp in combination with a monochromator. The separation efficiency was calculated according to the equation:
η
sep
=
J
H
2
O
2
/
J
abs
, in which JH2O2 and Jabs refer to the photocurrent density for PEC H2O2 oxidation and the integrated photocurrent density assuming all absorbed photons are converted to current, respectively. The injection efficiency was calculated by the formula:
η
inj
=
J
H
2
O
/
J
H
2
O
2
, in which JH2O and JH2O2 are the photocurrent density for PEC water oxidation and PEC H2O2 oxidation, respectively. The photoelectrochemical impedance spectra (PEIS) under AM 1.5 G illumination were measured over a frequency range of 100000–0.05 Hz at 1.0 V vs. RHE.

In order to explore the structure of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3, the crystalline structure of the obtained Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes was explored by XRD, as shown in Fig. S1. For the pristine Ti-Fe2O3, apart from the characteristic peaks of FTO, there are two diffraction peaks at 35.6° and 63.9°, corresponding to the (110) and (300) planes of α-Fe2O3 (PDF#33–0664). However, no additional diffraction peaks are observed for C/Ti-Fe2O3, NiFe-LDH/Ti-Fe2O3 and NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3, in comparison with the pristine Ti-Fe2O3. This might be because the content of CQDs and NiFe-LDH is below the lower limit of XRD detection [32].

Besides, the surface morphology of Ti-Fe2O3 and NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 was measured by FE-SEM. As displayed in Fig. 1(a) and (b), FTO is covered by uniform and dense nanorods for Ti-Fe2O3 and NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3, and the diameter of nanorods for NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 is slightly larger than that of Ti-Fe2O3, which is resulted from the impact of CQDs and NiFe-LDH. In addition, Fig. S2 shows the thickness of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 is about 500 nm, which is in line with that of Ti-Fe2O3. That is the deposition of CQDs and NiFe-LDH has little impact on the thickness of photoanode. Furthermore, the TEM image of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 in Fig. 1(c) indicates an amorphous layer is formed outside the surface of nanorod, which might be NiFe-LDH according to the reported literatures [16]. Meanwhile, there are some ultra-small particles on the surface of Ti-Fe2O3 nanorod, circled by yellow circles. The corresponding HRTEM image confirms the ultra-small particle with the lattice fringe spacing of 0.23 nm corresponds to CQDs [33], and the nanorod with the lattice fringe spacing of 0.22 nm corresponds to α-Fe2O3 [34], as illustrated in Fig. 1(d).

Moreover, it was further confirmed by XPS that NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 is synthesized as expected. The XPS results of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 are presented in Fig. 2. The C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2(a)) is fitted into three peaks centered at 284.7 eV, 286.3 eV and 288.3 eV, which are assigned to C-C, C-O and CO in CQDs, respectively [6]. In the O 1s XPS spectrum, the peak located at 532.6 eV corresponds to OC-O* , further proving the appearance of CQDs in NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 [35]. Besides, two additional peaks are observed in the O 1s XPS spectrum, including lattice oxygen (O2-) at 530.1 eV and surface-adsorbed hydroxyl oxygen (OH-) at 531.6 eV [36]. Fig. 2(c) shows Ni 2p1/2 at 873.9 eV and Ni 2p3/2 at 856.3 eV accompanied by a satellite peak are detected, which are matched well with the characteristics of Ni2+ in NiFe-LDH [17]. Meanwhile, we have observed two peaks at 711.2 eV and 725.0 eV, as well as a satellite peak at 719.3 eV, confirming Fe existed in Ti-Fe2O3 and NiFe-LDH is Fe3+ [37]. In conclusion, the results of XPS demonstrate the successful decoration of CQDs and NiFe-LDH on Ti-Fe2O3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC performance of Ti-Fe2O3 and Ti-Fe2O3 with cocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para:
The PEC performance of all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes were characterized by the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves. In order to increase the donor density of α-Fe2O3, Ti is doped in α-Fe2O3 in this study. As shown in Fig. S3, the photocurrent density of Ti-Fe2O3 is enhanced obviously benefitting from the increased donor density. In addition, we have optimized the synthesis condition of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 to improve the PEC performance. Fig. S4 shows the LSV curves of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 with different volume of CQDs solution and different deposition condition of NiFe-LDH. The results indicate the optimum synthesis condition of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 is as follows: the volume of CQDs solution is 200 μL, the electrodeposition time of NiFe-LDH is 120 s, and the molar ratio of Ni(NO3)2.6H2O to FeCl2.4H2O is 1:1. Therefore, we have taken 120(50)NiFe-LDH/200C/Ti-Fe2O3 as a guide to explore the effect of NiFe-LDH and CQDs in the subsequent discussion. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the photocurrent density of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 is 2.89 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is higher than that of Ti-Fe2O3 (0.80 mA/cm2), C/Ti-Fe2O3 (0.73 mA/cm2), NiFe-LDH/Ti-Fe2O3 (1.92 mA/cm2). Meanwhile, Fig. S5 shows the onset potential of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 (0.86 V vs. RHE) is shifted negatively compared with that of Ti-Fe2O3 (1.10 V vs. RHE), C/Ti-Fe2O3 (1.18 V vs. RHE) and NiFe-LDH/Ti-Fe2O3 (1.04 V vs. RHE), respectively. This tendency shows that both NiFe-LDH and CQDs are indispensable to the PEC performance of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3. To our knowledge, applied bias photon-to-current efficiencies (ABPE) and incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) are necessary in the practical application of photoanodes. Therefore, we have calculated ABPE values according to the LSV curves using the equation:
ABPE = (
J
light
−
J
dark
) × ( 1.23 V −
V
RHE
) /
P
light
, as shown in Fig. 3(b). The ABPE value of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 can reach up to 0.29% at 1.03 V vs. RHE, but only 0.13% at 1.08 V vs. RHE and 0.03% at 1.12 V vs. RHE for CoPi/Ti-Fe2O3 and C/Ti-Fe2O3, respectively. Besides, we have determined IPCE values according to the formula:
IPCE = (
J × 1240 )
/
( P
mono
× λ
)
. Fig. 3(c) shows the IPCE value of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 is 68.4% at 390 nm, which is higher than 10.6%, 5.5% and 54.3% for Ti-Fe2O3, C/Ti-Fe2O3, and NiFe-LDH/Ti-Fe2O3. One point to make here that C/Ti-Fe2O3 displays slightly lower IPCE values than Ti-Fe2O3, resulting from the inferior photocurrent density of C/Ti-Fe2O3 at 1.23 V vs. RHE. Furthermore, we have measured the photocurrent stability of the obtained photoanodes. As in Fig. 3(d), the photocurrent density of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 looks zigzag in contrast with other three photoanodes, which might be ascribed to the formation and rupture of bubbles caused by the higher photocurrent density. Fortunately, the photocurrent density of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 could maintain about 88% of the initial photocurrent density after 2 h photostability measurement, comparable with that of Ti-Fe2O3 (87%). This phenomenon indicates the photostability of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 is free from the influence of CQDs and NiFe-LDH.

On the basis of the above results, it is clear that both CQDs and NiFe-LDH are indispensable for the PEC performance of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3. To insight into the role of CQDs and NiFe-LDH, we have explored the mechanism of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 according to the contributing factors of PEC performance, including light absorption efficiency, separation efficiency and injection efficiency [31]. Firstly, the light absorption properties were measured by the UV–vis diffusion absorption spectra (UV–vis DRS) and the corresponding Tauc analysis. As shown in Fig. S6, the absorption intensity and band edge of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 are almost identical with those of Ti-Fe2O3, C/Ti-Fe2O3 and NiFe-LDH/Ti-Fe2O3. In other word, the introduction of CQDs and NiFe-LDH has no evident effect on the absorption property of Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes.

Secondly, the separation efficiency of photogenerated charges was determined by the surface photovoltage (SPV) based on the lock-in amplifier. Fig. 4(a) shows all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes exhibit obvious response in the wavelength range from 300 nm to 600 nm, which is attributed to the band to band charge transfer of Ti-Fe2O3 [38]. And the SPV intensity of all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes is positive, indicating the photogenerated holes are accumulated at the surface of photoanodes [39,40]. Meanwhile, we can observe the SPV intensity of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 is much higher than that of other photoanodes, although C/Ti-Fe2O3 and NiFe-LDH/Ti-Fe2O3 have higher SPV intensity than Ti-Fe2O3. According to the reported literatures, the separation efficiency of photogenerated charges is proportional to the SPV intensity [41,42]. Therefore, the separation efficiencies of the obtained photoanodes are sorted as follows: NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 > NiFe-LDH/Ti-Fe2O3 > C/Ti-Fe2O3 > Ti-Fe2O3. The enhanced separation efficiency of photogenerated charges for NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 is also confirmed by the SPV transient measurement (Fig. 4(b)). In addition, the kinetic behavior of photogenerated charges has been reflected from the SPV transient measurement. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the response of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 is quietly different from that of other photoanodes. In detail, the photovoltage response of Ti-Fe2O3, C/Ti-Fe2O3 and NiFe-LDH/Ti-Fe2O3 quickly reaches one peak and then immediately decays to zero, while the photovoltage response of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 reaches one peak and then stabilizes for some time to another peak and finally declines to zero. The emergence of another peak is ascribed to the diffusion process [39,43]. Namely, the photogenerated electrons are transferred from the surface to the bulk of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 due to the concentration gradient, while the photogenerated holes are located at the surface of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3. Benefitting from the slow decay of photogenerated holes, the difference of photovoltage intensity between NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 and other photoanodes in SPV measurement is much higher than that in SPV transient measurement. In other word, there are more photogenerated holes accumulated at the surface of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 for waiting to react with H2O in contrast with Ti-Fe2O3, C/Ti-Fe2O3 and NiFe-LDH/Ti-Fe2O3. In order to further confirm the efficient charge separation of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3, we have also determined the separation efficiency by comparing the photocurrent density for PEC H2O2 oxidation with the integrated photocurrent density (Jabs, Fig. S7). As shown in Fig. 4(c), the separation efficiency of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 is higher than other three photoanodes, in high accordance with the results of SPV and SPV transient measurements. Therefore, the introduction of CQDs between Ti-Fe2O3 and NiFe-LDH is beneficial for the directed transfer of photogenerated holes from Ti-Fe2O3 to the surface of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3.

Subsequently, with the purpose of further exploring the photogenerated charge behavior, we have carried out the transient photocurrent response of the obtained photoanodes under chopped illumination ( Fig. 5(a)), which is usually regarded as a useful method to study the hole accumulation process at the surface of photoanode [44]. In addition, the density of accumulated photogenerated holes at the different potential (Fig. 5(b)) is calculated by integrating the anodic photocurrent peak. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the trend of photocurrent density is consistent with that under continuous illumination. Meanwhile, the anodic photocurrent peaks are observed for all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes when the light is on, which is attributed to the accumulated photogenerated holes that transfer to the surface but do not react with H2O [44,45]. At low potentials (< 0.9 V vs. RHE), NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 exhibits obvious anodic photocurrent peaks as well as increased accumulated photogenerated holes than other photoanodes. At the moment, because the Faraday reaction (water oxidation reaction) is negligible, the anodic photocurrent peak is mainly resulted from the amount of photogenerated holes that transfer from Ti-Fe2O3 to the surface of photoanode. Therefore, we can make an inference that more photogenerated holes are migrated to the surface for NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3, in consistent with the results of SPV, SPV transient measurement and separation efficiency. Additionally, the weaker anodic photocurrent peaks and reduced accumulated photogenerated holes of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 at high potentials are also observed in Fig. 5(a-b). This phenomenon is a synergistic effect between the separated photogenerated holes and the injected photogenerated holes. In other word, the photogenerated holes of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 used in water oxidation reaction are increased in comparison with other photoanodes, on basis of the highest separation efficiency of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3. The injection efficiency of photogenerated holes is determined by the photocurrent density ratio in 1 M KOH with or without 0.5 M H2O2 [46]. Fig. 5(c) demonstrates that the injection efficiency of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 is highest among the obtained photoanodes, which further proves that there are more photogenerated holes to react with H2O. Surprisingly, NiFe-LDH/Ti-Fe2O3 also possesses superior injection efficiency of photogenerated holes, confirming the vital function of NiFe-LDH as a cocatalyst in improving the injection efficiency. For deeply understanding the role of NiFe-LDH on the charge transfer at the surface of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3, we have carried out the LSV curves in dark condition to investigate the electrocatalytic ability. Fig. S8 shows the electrocatalytic water oxidation performance is enhanced obviously after the deposition of NiFe-LDH, which also indicates NiFe-LDH as a cocatalyst could accelerate the water oxidation kinetics of Ti-Fe2O3.

To gain insight into the charge transport properties of all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes, the photoelectrochemical impedance spectra (PEIS) were conducted at 1.0 V vs. RHE and the Nyquist plots were simulated by an equivalent circuit ( Fig. 6). The bode plots (Fig. S9) exhibit there are two arcs for all Ti-Fe2O3-based photoanodes, making it possible to simulate the data by the same equivalent circuit. In the Nyquist plots, the radius of arcs at low frequency hints the charge transfer at the photoanode/electrolyte interface, while the radius of arcs at high frequency represents the charge transfer in the bulk photoanode [47]. On the one hand, in contrast with Ti-Fe2O3 and C/Ti-Fe2O3, the semicircle diameters of NiFe-LDH/Ti-Fe2O3 and NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 at low frequency are obviously decreased as shown in Fig. 6(a). Besides, Table 1 demonstrates the resistance at the photoanode/electrolyte interface (Rct2) follows the trend: NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 < NiFe-LDH/Ti-Fe2O3 < < C/Ti-Fe2O3 < Ti-Fe2O3, indicating the charge transfer at the photoanode/electrolyte interface is facilitated for NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 and NiFe-LDH/Ti-Fe2O3. This conclusion further confirms the electrocatalytic performance of NiFe-LDH, in agreement with the results of injection efficiency and LSV curves in dark condition. In addition, the CPE2 value of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 is highest, followed by NiFe-LDH/Ti-Fe2O3, C/Ti-Fe2O3 and Ti-Fe2O3. It reveals that the photogenerated holes arrived at the surface of photoanodes are more than other obtained photoanodes, because the CPE2 values are positively associated with the amount of photogenerated holes at the surface of photoanodes [48]. On the other hand, it is observed that the modification of CQDs or NiFe-LDH decreases the transfer resistance in the bulk photoanodes (Rct1), which is beneficial for the photogenerated holes transfer from the valence band of Ti-Fe2O3 to the surface of photoanode. However, the Rct1 value of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 is increased slightly than NiFe-LDH/Ti-Fe2O3 and C/Ti-Fe2O3, although it is lower than the pristine Ti-Fe2O3. As for why this happens is that the multiple interfaces are introduced when CQDs and NiFe-LDH are co-deposited on Ti-Fe2O3. Even so, there might be more photogenerated holes accumulated at the surface of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3, resulting from the significantly enhanced separation efficiency.

Taken together, we have come a conclusion that the higher PEC performance of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 could be attributed to the following reasons. Firstly, the incorporation of CQDs between Ti-Fe2O3 and NiFe-LDH facilitates the charge separation and prolongs the recombination time of photogenerated charges, and thus the amount of photogenerated holes at the surface of photoanode waiting for participating into water oxidation reaction is enhanced. Secondly, the excellent electrocatalytic performance of NiFe-LDH plays a vital role in improving the injection efficiency of photogenerated holes, and significantly decreases the transfer resistance of photogenerated holes at the interface of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 and electrolyte.

In summary, we have successfully fabricated NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 as a photoanode applied in PEC water oxidation reaction. In contrast with Ti-Fe2O3, the resultant NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 shows a higher and stable photocurrent density of 2.89 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, as well as a lower onset potential of 0.86 V vs. RHE. In addition, our results clearly indicate there are two factors attributing to the superior PEC performance, namely, the high-efficient separation of photogenerated charges in the bulk photoanode and rapid injection of photogenerated holes at the photoanode/electrolyte interface under the synergistic action of CQDs and NiFe-LDH. The demonstration of NiFe-LDH/C/Ti-Fe2O3 herein paves a way toward the design of highly active photoanodes with low-cost.

Qijing Bu: Investigation, Methodology, Experiment, Writing-original draft. Qifeng Zhao: Investigation, Validation. Gaohao Sun: Validation, Formal analysis. Qingyun Liu: Writing-review & editing, Supervision. Tengfeng Xie: Writing-review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21773086, 21872063).

ce:section.ce:para: The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2022.164643.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
